section.title: Children of God
section.sectionRationale: John lifts the readers' eyes to their identity and destiny: they are now children of God through the Father's astonishing love, and one day they will be like Christ. That hope purifies them, and John shows that those born of God practice righteousness while those of the devil practice sin, drawing a clear line between the two families.
section.thesis: Because the Father has lavished His love in making believers His children now, and because they will be like Christ when He appears, they purify themselves and practice righteousness, for the Son appeared to take away sins and destroy the devil's works, and everyone born of God, in whom God's seed abides, does not go on living in sin.
section.frameNote: Four beats: remain in Christ to be confident at His coming; the love that makes us children now and the hope of being like Him; sin as lawlessness that Christ came to take away; and the two families distinguished by righteousness and love.

verses.text: And now, little children, {{meno|remain}} in Christ, so that when He appears, we may be confident and unashamed before Him at His {{parousia|coming}}.
If you know that He is righteous, you also know that everyone who practices righteousness has been {{gennao|born}} of Him.
Behold what manner of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called {{teknon|children}} of God. And that is what we are! The reason the world does not know us is that it did not know Him.
Beloved, we are now children of God, and what we will be has not yet been revealed. We know that when Christ appears, we will be like Him, for we will see Him as He is.
And everyone who has this hope in Him {{hagnizo|purifies}} himself, just as Christ is pure.

units.label: Sin is lawlessness
units.note: Christ appeared to take away sins

verses.text: Everyone who practices sin practices {{anomia|lawlessness}} as well. Indeed, sin is lawlessness.
But you know that Christ appeared to take away sins, and in Him there is no sin.
No one who remains in Him keeps on sinning. No one who continues to sin has seen Him or known Him.
Little children, let no one deceive you: The one who practices righteousness is righteous, just as Christ is righteous.
The one who practices sin is of the devil, because the devil has been sinning from the very start. This is why the Son of God was revealed, to destroy the works of the devil.
Anyone born of God refuses to practice sin, because God's {{sperma|seed}} abides in him; he cannot go on sinning, because he has been born of God.
By this the children of God are distinguished from the children of the devil: Anyone who does not practice righteousness is not of God, nor is anyone who does not love his brother.

meno.gloss: To remain, abide, continue, dwell. John's key word for lasting union with Christ. To remain in Him is to stay in living fellowship, and it is the ground of confidence when Christ appears, so that believers will not shrink back in shame at His coming.

parousia.gloss: Coming, arrival, presence. It became the standard term for the future coming of Christ in glory. Believers who remain in Him can await His parousia with confidence rather than shame, since they belong to Him and will be made like Him.

gennao.gloss: To beget, give birth to; in the passive, to be born. To be born of God is the new birth, a real change of family and nature. It is God's work, not ours, and it shows itself in a life of righteousness; the children reflect the character of the Father who gave them life.

teknon.gloss: Child, offspring, one born into a family. John marvels that we are called children of God, and insists it is not a mere title: that is what we are. It speaks of belonging, likeness, and inheritance, a status given purely by the Father's love.

hagnizo.gloss: To purify, make clean, consecrate. Everyone who has the hope of being like Christ purifies himself, pursuing holiness now in view of who he will be. It is an active, ongoing cleansing shaped by the pattern of Christ's own purity, not a claim to be already flawless.

anomia.gloss: Lawlessness, the state or deed of being against God's law. John says sin is lawlessness, a rebellion against God's rightful order and rule. It exposes sin as not merely weakness but defiance, which is why a settled life of sin is incompatible with being born of God.

sperma.gloss: Seed, offspring, that which is sown. God's seed abides in the one born of God, an image for the divine life or word implanted at the new birth. Because this seed remains, the child of God cannot go on living in sin; the new nature bears the family likeness.

basic.context: John points the readers to who they are: children of God, made so by the Father's amazing love. That is not just a nice title; it is what they truly are. And there is more coming: when Christ appears, they will be like Him. This hope makes them want to be pure now. John then draws a clear line: those born of God practice righteousness and love, because God's life is in them, while those who go on living in sin belong to the devil, whose works Christ came to destroy.
basic.hermeneutics: The hard verses are 3:6 and 3:9, that no one born of God keeps on sinning. Read alongside 1:8-10, which says anyone claiming to have no sin is a liar, John is not teaching sinless perfection. The Greek suggests a settled, ongoing practice of sin, a whole direction of life. A child of God does not live in unbroken, unrepentant sin, because God's seed remains in him, even though he still sins and confesses.
basic.originalLanguages: Believers are to remain, meno, in Christ so they are confident at His coming, parousia. They are born, gennao, of God and called His children, teknon. The hope of being like Him leads each to purify, hagnizo, himself. Sin is lawlessness, anomia, but God's seed, sperma, abides in the one born of God, so he does not go on sinning.
basic.typology: The image of being born of God and bearing His seed fulfils the promise of a new heart and new spirit given to God's people. Being made children who will be like the Son answers the calling of humanity to bear God's image, now restored and completed in Christ.
basic.christ: The Son of God appeared to take away sins and to destroy the works of the devil, and in Him there is no sin. Believers will be made like Him when they see Him as He is, and even now they purify themselves after the pattern of His purity. He is both the Righteous One from whom they are born and the goal into whose likeness they are being remade.
basic.god: The Father's love is lavish and transforming, making rebels into His own children. He is righteous, and His children bear His righteous likeness. He implants His own seed of life in them so that they cannot go on living in sin, showing a God who not only forgives but truly changes those He adopts.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:12-13 says those who receive Christ are born of God. Romans 8 speaks of adoption and future glory with Christ. Ezekiel 36 promises a new heart and spirit, and 1 John 3:8 names Christ's purpose to destroy the devil's works.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's story of God gathering a family reaches a height here: by love the Father makes sinners His children, implants His life in them, and promises to make them like His Son. The Son came to undo the devil's works and take away sin, so the children of God are being remade into the likeness the whole story has aimed at from the beginning.

context.literary: The section pivots on the family language of children, birth, and seed, framed by the appearing of Christ, at the start, 2:28, and the destroying of the devil's works. It alternates wonder, 'Behold what manner of love', with sharp moral contrast between the two families, building to the summary in 3:10.
context.historical: The false teachers apparently treated sin as unimportant for the spiritually enlightened. John counters that those born of God cannot go on living in sin, and that a life of unrepentant sin marks one as belonging to the devil, not to God, however loud the spiritual claims.
context.canonical: The passage draws on the new-birth teaching of John's Gospel and the adoption and glorification themes of Romans 8, and on the prophetic promise of a new heart, Ezekiel 36. It looks ahead to the appearing of Christ when His people will be transformed into His likeness, 1 Corinthians 15, Philippians 3.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure believers of their identity as God's beloved children and their destiny to be like Christ, to fuel present holiness by that hope, and to distinguish the children of God, marked by righteousness and love, from the children of the devil, marked by a settled life of sin.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes what believers are, children of God, and will be, like Christ, and it prescribes the fitting response, remain in Him, purify yourself, practice righteousness, love your brother. The statements about the two families describe the fruit that reveals to which family a person belongs.

debates.question: How can John say 'no one born of God keeps on sinning' (3:6, 3:9) when he also says anyone claiming to have no sin is a liar (1:8-10)?

views.label: It describes a settled practice of sin, not the presence of any sin
views.case: The Greek present tense in 3:6 and 3:9 points to ongoing, habitual, characteristic sinning, a whole direction of life, not the occasional sins every believer still commits. Because God's seed abides in the one born of God, he cannot go on living in unrepentant sin as his settled pattern, though he still stumbles and confesses, 1:8-10. This reading harmonises the two passages and fits the letter as a whole, and it is the strongest and most widely held view.

views.label: Believers can and must reach sinless perfection now
views.case: Some take the words absolutely to mean the born-again do not sin at all; but this flatly contradicts 1:8 and 1:10 and John's provision of an advocate for when we sin, 2:1.

views.label: John speaks of an ideal never realised in this life
views.case: Others treat it as an unattainable ideal held out for aspiration; but John states it as a present reality of those born of God, not a mere ideal.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading is that a child of God does not live in unbroken, unrepentant sin, because God's implanted life truly changes him, even while he still sins and must confess. This guards both against the perfectionism 1:8-10 denies and against the false teachers' indifference to sin, giving real assurance grounded in a changed life and a faithful advocate.

study.originalLanguages: John's charge is to meno, to remain in Christ, so that at His parousia, His coming in glory, believers will stand confident rather than ashamed. They have been gennao, born of God, and so are His teknon, His children, a status of belonging and likeness given by the Father's love. The hope of being like Christ moves each one to hagnizo, to purify himself, after the pattern of Christ's own purity. Sin is unmasked as anomia, lawlessness, defiance of God's rule, but the one born of God has God's sperma, His seed, His very life, abiding within, and so cannot go on making sin the settled practice of his life.

typology.type: Being born of God with His seed abiding within
typology.fulfillment: The promised new heart and new spirit, Ezekiel 36:26-27, given in the new birth so that God's people walk in His ways from the inside out.

typology.type: Children of God to be made like the Son
typology.fulfillment: Humanity's calling to bear God's image, marred at the fall and restored in Christ, into whose likeness His people will be transformed when He appears, 1 Corinthians 15:49, Philippians 3:21.

study.christ: The Son of God appeared with a twofold purpose named here: to take away sins, for in Him there is no sin, and to destroy the works of the devil. He is the Righteous One from whom His people are born and whose likeness they bear, and He is the goal of their hope, for when He appears they will be like Him, seeing Him as He is. Present holiness is shaped by His purity, and final transformation is into His image, so Christ is both the source and the summit of the children's life.
study.god: The Father's love is not merely sentimental but creative and transforming: it makes rebels into His own children and is lavished so freely that John cries, Behold what manner of love. God is righteous, and He implants His own seed of life in His children so that they bear His likeness and cannot go on living in sin. He does not only pardon; He regenerates, adopts, and will one day glorify, showing a God committed to remaking His people entirely.

crossReferences.note: To all who received Him He gave the right to become children of God, born not of blood but of God, the new birth John assumes here.
Those led by the Spirit are children of God and heirs with Christ, matching the identity and inheritance John celebrates.
God promises a new heart and His Spirit within, the implanted life pictured by God's seed abiding in the believer.
As we have borne the image of the man of dust, we will bear the image of the heavenly Man, the being like Christ John promises.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible tells of God gathering a family and restoring His image in humanity. Here that purpose blazes out: by sheer love the Father makes sinners His children, plants His own life within them, and pledges to make them like His Son when He appears. The Son came to take away sin and destroy the devil's works, so the children of God are even now being remade in righteousness and love, moving toward the likeness of Christ that has been the story's aim from the start.

sermon.title: The Children of God
sermon.why: Begg preaches this section, 1 John 2:28-3:3, on the Father's love in making us His children, the hope of being like Christ, and the purity that hope produces. A clear, orthodox handling of Christian identity and destiny.

quiz.q: What does John marvel at in 1 John 3:1?

options.text: The manner of love the Father gave, that we should be called children of God, which is what we truly are.
options.note: John stands amazed at the Father's love in making believers His actual children.

options.text: That believers deserve to be called God's children by their effort.
options.note: It is the Father's love given to us, not something earned.

options.text: That the world admires and welcomes God's children.
options.note: John says the opposite; the world does not know us because it did not know Him.

options.text: That being a child of God is only a future hope, not a present reality.
options.note: John insists it is present, we are now children of God, though fuller glory is still to come.

quiz.q: What does John say believers will be when Christ appears?

options.text: Like Him, for we will see Him as He is.
options.note: Verse 3:2 promises transformation into Christ's likeness at His appearing.

options.text: Exactly the same as they are now, with no change.
options.note: John says what we will be has not yet been revealed; we will be like Him.

options.text: Absorbed into God so as to lose all individuality.
options.note: The promise is likeness to Christ, not loss of personhood.

options.text: Rewarded according to their nationality.
options.note: The transformation is into Christ's likeness for all His children, not by nationality.

quiz.q: Why did the Son of God appear, according to verse 8?

options.text: To destroy the works of the devil.
options.note: Verse 8 names the purpose: the Son came to undo the devil's works.

options.text: To leave humanity to sort out sin themselves.
options.note: He came to act decisively against sin and the devil, not to leave us alone.

options.text: To offer good advice about self-improvement.
options.note: He came to take away sins and destroy the devil's works, far more than advice.

options.text: To reward the righteous and ignore everyone else.
options.note: He came to take away sins and defeat the devil, opening the way for sinners to become children of God.

quiz.q: How should we understand 'no one born of God keeps on sinning' alongside 'if we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves'?

options.text: It means a settled, unrepentant practice of sin, not the presence of any sin at all.
options.note: The present tense points to habitual sinning; believers still sin and confess, 1:8-10.

options.text: Believers can reach total sinless perfection in this life.
options.note: This contradicts 1:8-10 and the advocate provided in 2:1.

options.text: Sin does not matter for those who are born again.
options.note: John takes sin seriously; his point is that God's children do not live in it.

options.text: It is only an ideal that no one ever actually reaches.
options.note: John states it as a present reality of the born-again, not merely an unreachable ideal.

quiz.q: How are the children of God distinguished from the children of the devil (verse 10)?

options.text: By practicing righteousness and loving their brothers.
options.note: Verse 10 names righteousness and love as the marks of God's children.

options.text: By having no visible differences at all.
options.note: John says there is a clear distinction, seen in righteousness and love.

options.text: By outward religious reputation alone.
options.note: The mark is real righteousness and love, not mere reputation.

options.text: By strict rule-keeping without any love.
options.note: Righteousness matters, but John binds it with love for the brothers; rule-keeping without love is not the mark.

questions: John says, Behold what manner of love, that we are called children of God. How does resting in this given identity change the way you see yourself on a hard day?
The hope of being like Christ leads believers to purify themselves. How does what you will be one day shape the choices you make now?
The Son appeared to destroy the works of the devil. Where do you most long to see His victory over sin and evil in your own life?
God's seed abides in those born of Him, so they cannot go on living in sin. How do you tell the difference between a believer who stumbles and confesses and a life settled in unrepentant sin?
The children of God are marked by righteousness and love. Which of these two marks is God pressing on you to grow in right now, and how?

translationNotes.note: 'When Christ appears' can also read 'when it appears', as the BSB footnote notes; the sense is the same, the future manifestation at which believers are transformed into His likeness.
'Keeps on sinning' and 'continues to sin' render present tenses indicating ongoing, habitual practice, which is why it does not contradict 1:8-10, where believers still commit and confess sins.
'God's seed abides in him' pictures the divine life or word implanted at the new birth; because it remains, the child of God cannot make sin the settled practice of his life.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 2:28-3:10. No em dashes in this passage. Footnotes at 3:2, 3:3, 3:4, and 3:7 noted; variant renderings do not change the sense.
